---
title: "Lecture 4: Machine Learning"
subtitle: >
  {{< var course.code >}}, {{< var course.term >}}
number-sections: false
---

## Lecture overview
### Learning outcomes
- TODO

### Pre-class work
::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student" when-profile="ta"}
#### Instructors
1. TODO

#### Coordinators
1. TODO
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student"}
#### TAs
1. TODO
:::

#### Learners
1. TODO

### Lecture timeline
::: {.list-table}
- - Topic
  - Time
  - Materials

- - [Differences from previous lectures](#sec-topic-1)
  - 10 min

- - [Credit card fraud detection](#sec-topic-2)
    - Features and labels
    - Parameters
  - 20 min

- - [Activity: Optimization](#sec-topic-3)
  - 20 min
  - Tool TODO

- - [Google Teachable Machine](#sec-topic-4)
  - 10 min
  - [Google Teachable Machine](https://teachablemachine.withgoogle.com/train/image)
:::

### Post-class work
::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student" when-profile="ta"}
#### Instructors
1. TODO

#### Coordinators
1. TODO
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student"}
#### TAs
1. TODO
:::

#### Learners
1. TODO

## Differences from previous lectures{#sec-topic-1}
- We do not want to use pre-defined, set rules like we did in explicit rule-based programming.
- In machine learning, we build a model that will learn the rules from the data.

### Activity: Discussion
- What real-life applications of machine learning do you know?

## Credit card fraud detection{#sec-topic-2}
- Have you ever had your credit card declined on vacation? You were probably interacting with a credit card fraud detection model!
- Being able to identify fraudulent transactions is useful: it protects businesses and credit card holders from financial loss after fraud attempts happen.
- Billions of transactions are processed every day, so being able to identify these transactions programmatically is even better.

### Activity: Discussion
- What information could be used to identify whether a transaction is fraudulent or not?

### Features and Labels
- Data used to train a credit card fraud detection model could include:

| User ID | Amount | Location | Time | Fraud? |
| ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- |
| 1 | $100 | Vancouver | 10:00 AM | No |
| 2 | $20 | Burnaby | 2:00 PM | No |
| 3 | $1500 | New York | 11:00 PM | Yes |

- Each row in the table above is an **example**. Each example has **features** and **labels**. 
  - Features are things we already know about an example, like User ID, Amount, Location, and Time.
  - Labels are things we would like to predict, like Fraud.
  - We represent the features with X and represent the labels with Y. [TODO: is it important that they know this?]
- We **train** the model on lots of examples with labels we already know so that it can learn to **predict** labels for new examples.
- If you have lots of features, manually coming up with a rule for every possible example the model might see is not reasonable! With machine learning, the model learns the rules for you.
  - What kind of rules does the model learn? How do the rules relate to the examples?

### Activity: Discussion
- How would you use each feature in the table above to predict fraud?

### Parameters
- The model needs to decide **how important each feature is** to the prediction.
  - The location of a transaction will probably be more useful for predicting fraud than the user's credit card number.
- The model also needs to decide **how far each feature "pushes" the prediction towards each label**. 
  - A large transaction will probably push the prediction towards fraud, while a small transaction will probably push the prediction towards not fraud. [TODO: might be easier to think about with binary features?]
- No single feature decides whether a transaction is fraudulent or not: each feature contributes to the overall prediction.
- [TODO: discuss feature interactions being more informative than individual features?]
- This relationship between features and labels is stored in the model as **parameters**.

## Activity: Optimization{#sec-topic-3}
- How do we find these parameters? What do we mean by **optimization**?
- TODO activity - needs a tool

## Google Teachable Machine{#sec-topic-4}
- TODO formalize instructions
- [TODO: any issues with using the webcam/images of prof or students? alternatively have a set of images ready to upload]
- have prof or volunteer student come up to front and use webcam to record images of them doing 2 distinct things (smiling/frowning, left/right of screen, wearing hat/not wearing hat, etc). train model, then test whether it can differentiate the 2 distinct things
- with ~100 images per class and 2 classes, takes ~2 min to train. do not switch tabs during training as it will stop
- after demonstration at front, could let students try on their own:
  - add more classes
  - train with object added to/removed from background between two different unrelated classes (e.g. smiling with water bottle, frowning without) and see how adding/removing the object during testing changes the accuracy
  - train with one person smiling/frowning and use another person smiling/frowning to test
    - see if model improves if both people are used to train
  - do something that wasn't in either class and see which class it is more like (e.g. shocked face). why do you think that is/what features probably make that the case? (e.g. if shocked is most like smiling, eyebrow movements might be similar, etc...)

[TODO: check length of this lecture]
